Part B.key_evidence: The difference cutout shows a compact positive residual at the exact center, and the science cutout shows a point-like source there that is not evident in the template. The residual looks localized rather than like a broad subtraction failure. Metadata show ndethist=1 and deltajd=0 despite ncovhist=1650, and there is no close PS1 counterpart at the candidate position (nearest PS1 source is 17.35 arcsec away).
Part B.leading_interpretation_and_support: This is most consistent with a real solar-system object (Asteroid in this benchmark). The source is point-like, appears only in the science image, lacks a persistent counterpart or host at the alert position, and has only a single same-position detection. That pattern is more naturally explained by a moving object crossing blank sky than by a supernova, AGN, or stellar variable tied to a fixed source in the template.
Part B.alternative_analysis: A plausible alternative is a hostless or very-faint-host supernova, since the subtraction is positive and point-like. However, the absence of any obvious host or persistent source at the center, plus the single-detection history, makes the asteroid interpretation stronger. Variable star and AGN are disfavored because there is no clear fixed counterpart at the alert position.
